evidence: BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor performs heavy I/O at construction time: 'this.blackLabIndex = BlackLab.open(new File(indexPath))' — this opens a BlackLab index which involves file system access, memory mapping, and potentially significant latency. This is declared throws IOException, so failures surface eagerly, but it couples object creation to I/O availability.

SemanticFieldExplorer(String indexPath) convenience constructor creates a new BlackLabQueryExecutor(indexPath) internally. In Main.handleServerCommand, the server already creates 'QueryExecutor executor = new BlackLabQueryExecutor(indexPath)' and passes it to WordSketchApiServer. WordSketchApiServer then creates SemanticFieldExplorer(executor) using DI — correct path. However, the String-path constructor exists and is callable, risking double-open if used outside this path.

SemanticFieldExplorer constructor comment: 'Prefer SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor) (dependency injection) for testability and to avoid double-open of the same index (see issue 297b2b52).' — the risk is acknowledged but the constructor is not guarded.

GrammarConfigLoader constructor reads the grammar JSON file at construction time (Files.readString or Files.exists + Files.readString). This is appropriate I/O for a config loader; it is declared throws IOException.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() calls System.getProperty('grammar.config', 'grammars/relations.json') — this reads a system property at call time (a static factory method), not at class-load time. No class-level static initializers read environment state.

No module-level (class-level static initializer) side effects observed: no static { } blocks performing I/O, no static fields initialized via database/network/file calls, no circular init dependencies.
BlackLabSnippetParser static final Pattern fields (LEMMA_ATTR, XPOS_ATTR, etc.) are compiled at class-load time — pure computation, no I/O, no external state dependencies.
LongIntHashMap, LogDiceCalculator, CqlUtils, and PosGroup have no static state beyond constants.

Main.handleServerCommand creates BlackLabQueryExecutor and GrammarConfigLoader sequentially at startup, both outside any static context — initialization is ordered and explicit, not hidden in class loading.

strengths: No class-level static initializers perform I/O or read environment state — all side-effectful initialization happens in constructors or factory methods called explicitly at startup.
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads System.getProperty at call time in a static factory, not at class load time, avoiding fragile import-time environment coupling.
Main.handleServerCommand establishes a clear, ordered initialization sequence: executor → grammarConfig → server, each step explicit and exception-safe.
All classes with I/O in constructors (BlackLabQueryExecutor, GrammarConfigLoader) declare throws IOException, making failures visible rather than swallowed.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The primary risk is not a classic import-time side-effect but a constructor-time heavy I/O operation (BlackLab index open) combined with a convenience constructor on SemanticFieldExplorer that can create a second index handle over the same path — a known risk acknowledged in comments but not enforced.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: This codebase avoids the worst initialization-coupling patterns: no static-block I/O, no circular lazy imports, no env-var reads at class load time. The dominant concern is the SemanticFieldExplorer dual-constructor risk (double-open) and the general pattern of constructors doing heavy I/O, which reduces testability and makes boot failures opaque. These are real but manageable risks rather than systemic initialization coupling. The score reflects a codebase that handles initialization better than average but still carries a well-documented unresolved risk around index ownership.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer(String indexPath) can create a second BlackLabQueryExecutor over the same index, risking resource contention
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer(String indexPath): 'this.executor = new BlackLabQueryExecutor(indexPath); this.ownsExecutor = true;' — creates a second independent BlackLabIndex handle.

Main.handleServerCommand creates BlackLabQueryExecutor(indexPath) explicitly, then passes it to WordSketchApiServer which calls new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor) — correct. But SemanticFieldExplorer(String) remains callable.
Code comment: 'Prefer SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor) to avoid double-open of the same index (see issue 297b2b52).' — the risk is documented but not prevented.
issues.suggestion: Mark SemanticFieldExplorer(String indexPath) @Deprecated with a Javadoc @deprecated note. In the constructor body, add a warning log: logger.warn("SemanticFieldExplorer(String) creates an owned index handle; prefer SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor) to share the existing handle."). Audit all callers; if none use it in production, remove it. If removal is deferred, the deprecation marker at least makes the risk visible in IDEs.

issues.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutor opens the BlackLab index in its constructor, coupling object creation to I/O availability and making the object untestable without a real index
evidence: Constructor body: 'this.blackLabIndex = BlackLab.open(new File(indexPath));' wrapped in try-catch Exception → IOException. BlackLab.open involves file system traversal, Lucene segment loading, and memory mapping.

The QueryExecutor interface is used in tests (SemanticFieldExplorerTest, HybridAlgorithmTest) but BlackLabQueryExecutor itself is only tested via BlackLabDummyTest with a real index path — confirming that the I/O-in-constructor design forces integration tests over unit tests.
issues.suggestion: Consider a factory method pattern: make the constructor package-private and add a static BlackLabQueryExecutor open(String indexPath) throws IOException factory. This makes the I/O intent explicit in callers ('open' reads as I/O; 'new' does not) and opens the door for a create-uninitialized path useful in testing. As a lower-effort alternative, add a Javadoc note on the constructor: '@throws IOException if the index cannot be opened — this constructor performs file I/O and should not be called in unit tests; use a mock QueryExecutor instead.'

root_causes: The dual-constructor design on SemanticFieldExplorer was a convenience affordance that was not deprecated when the DI constructor became the preferred path — a common pattern where the old way is never formally retired.

Java's constructor naming convention does not signal I/O operations ('new BlackLabQueryExecutor' looks lightweight until you know what BlackLab.open does); a factory method would make the I/O intent visible at call sites.
likely_symptoms: sef-string-constructor-double-open-risk is a specific symptom of not enforcing ownership rules at the API level.
possible_false_positives: GrammarConfigLoader constructor doing file I/O — appropriate for a config loader, not an initialization coupling issue.
BlackLabSnippetParser static Pattern fields — pure compile-time constants, not import-time side effects.
